fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Fixing x,y:A we must show that \mathsf {act}_{f}\colon x = y \to fx=fy is invertible. By and , it suffices to exhibit a biinverse to \mathsf {act}_{f} (it is convenient that the underlying maps of the section and retraction need not be identical).

We first define the sections s_{x,y}\colon fx = fy \to x = y. Because f is assumed to be propositional, we have by an assignment \phi _{x,y}: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} \cong _{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen y,h^{-1}\right \rparen \mathclose {} . We therefore define s_{x,y}h :\equiv \mathopen {}\left \lparen \phi _{x,y}h\right \rparen \mathclose {} .1; the witness of sectionhood can be constructed using \mathopen {}\left \lparen \phi _{x,y}h\right \rparen \mathclose {} .2 modulo some identification algebra.

To construct the retractions r_{x,y}\colon fx=fy\to x = y, we note by our assumption that we have an assignment \psi _{x,y}:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} =_{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen y,h^{-1}\right \rparen \mathclose {} , and we define r_{x,y}h :\equiv \mathsf {act}_{-.1} \mathopen {}\left \lparen \psi _{x,y}h\right \rparen \mathclose {} . It remains to show that for all x,y:A and h\colon x =y, we have \mathsf {act}_{-.1} \mathopen {}\left \lparen \psi _{x,y}h\right \rparen \mathclose {} =h. By induction on h it suffices to show that for all x:A we have \mathsf {act}_{-.1} \mathopen {}\left \lparen \psi _{x,x}\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} = \mathsf {refl}. We will deduce that \psi _{x,x}\mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl} from our assumption that f\colon A\to B is propositional and thus fibred in sets via .
Any propositional map f\colon A\to B is an embedding.

Fixing x,y:A we must show that \mathsf {act}_{f}\colon x = y \to fx=fy is invertible. By and , it suffices to exhibit a biinverse to \mathsf {act}_{f} (it is convenient that the underlying maps of the section and retraction need not be identical).

We first define the sections s_{x,y}\colon fx = fy \to x = y. Because f is assumed to be propositional, we have by an assignment \phi _{x,y}: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} \cong _{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen y,h^{-1}\right \rparen \mathclose {} . We therefore define s_{x,y}h :\equiv \mathopen {}\left \lparen \phi _{x,y}h\right \rparen \mathclose {} .1; the witness of sectionhood can be constructed using \mathopen {}\left \lparen \phi _{x,y}h\right \rparen \mathclose {} .2 modulo some identification algebra.

To construct the retractions r_{x,y}\colon fx=fy\to x = y, we note by our assumption that we have an assignment \psi _{x,y}:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen h:fx=fy\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} =_{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen y,h^{-1}\right \rparen \mathclose {} , and we define r_{x,y}h :\equiv \mathsf {act}_{-.1} \mathopen {}\left \lparen \psi _{x,y}h\right \rparen \mathclose {} . It remains to show that for all x,y:A and h\colon x =y, we have \mathsf {act}_{-.1} \mathopen {}\left \lparen \psi _{x,y}h\right \rparen \mathclose {} =h. By induction on h it suffices to show that for all x:A we have \mathsf {act}_{-.1} \mathopen {}\left \lparen \psi _{x,x}\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} = \mathsf {refl}. We will deduce that \psi _{x,x}\mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl} from our assumption that f\colon A\to B is propositional and thus fibred in sets via .
